draft.introduction: In De Anima II.1, Aristotle defines the soul as the first actuality of a natural body that has life potentially. The soul is not a separate substance but the form of the living body—the organizing principle that makes an organism what it is.

translation.text: The soul is the first actuality of a natural body having life potentially. This will apply to any body that possesses organs. The soul is the cause and principle of the living body. It is substance in the sense of the form of a natural body which has life potentially. Such substance is actuality. The soul, therefore, is the actuality of the kind of body described.
translation.original_text: ψυχή ἐστιν ἐντελέχεια ἡ πρώτη σώματος φυσικοῦ δυνάμει ζωὴν ἔχοντος

translator_notes.note: The technical term entelechia (ἐντελέχεια), coined by Aristotle, means “being-at-work-staying-itself”—the ongoing realization of a thing’s nature. This hylomorphic definition of the soul challenged the Platonic view of the soul as a separate, immortal substance.
